one_line_takeaway: Short natural-language or optimized trigger tokens can exploit Mamba/Mamba-2's recurrent hidden-state selectivity mechanism to irreversibly overwrite context ('Hidden State Poisoning'), degrading pure-SSM models by 71% CHSS on a new long-context retrieval benchmark and amplifying prompt-injection success x1.516 on a 52B production hybrid (Jamba-1.7-Mini), while pure Transformers are largely unaffected or even improve.
key_finding: Z-HiSPA triggers cause a 71% average CHSS drop for Mamba-2.8b (with recovery instruction) while the identical triggers cause a 58% average CHSS *increase* for Pythia-2.8b, demonstrating the vulnerability is architecture-specific to the SSM recurrence rather than a general instruction-following effect. Jamba-1.7-Mini (near-perfect clean CHSS ~0.99-1.0) is resistant to Z-HiSPA triggers but collapses to CHSS 0 under a specific GA-optimized M-HiSPA trigger containing the <|endoftext|> special token -- though this same trigger also collapses Pythia-2.8b, so the authors flag it as a confounded, not cleanly SSM-specific, result. Cleaner SSM-specific evidence comes from the PIA experiment: on OPEN-PROMPT-INJECTIONS, Jamba's attack success value (ASV) rises from 0.490 (naive) to 0.743 (combine trigger), a x1.516 relative increase, whereas Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct sees a smaller x1.265 increase and Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct actually becomes *less* vulnerable (x0.607, ASV drops from 0.272 to 0.165) under the same combine trigger. Mechanistic analysis found block 28-37 output-embedding L2 norms strongly negatively correlate with CHSS (r < -0.91 across the band, peak r = -0.9707 at block 29), supporting a proposed two-stage poisoning mechanism: first-block hidden-state saturation followed by mid-layer amplification. Optimizing GA triggers purely for first-block loss produced weaker ROBENCH-25 degradation than the hand-crafted Z-HiSPA triggers, despite lower loss values, indicating first-block saturation alone is necessary but not sufficient.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Hidden State Poisoning Attacks (HiSPA), a new adversarial attack class specific to Mamba/Mamba-2 state-space models (SSMs), in which a short trigger sequence exploits the SSM's selectivity mechanism to irreversibly overwrite (saturate) the recurrent hidden state h_t, causing 'partial amnesia' about prior context. The authors: (1) give a theoretical derivation (contraction bound argument) showing a short trigger of length L can be constructed so its input contribution dominates the carried-over hidden state, generalizing from Mamba to Mamba-2's scalar-identity transition; (2) build ROBENCH-25, a 120-NeurIPS-abstract long-context information-retrieval benchmark with True/False questions, scored via clipped Heidke skill score (CHSS); (3) define two attacker variants -- zero-shot black-box Z-HiSPA (natural-language triggers styled after known prompt-injection strategies) and white-box optimized M-HiSPA (genetic-algorithm-optimized token sequences targeting internal loss objectives); (4) run a mechanistic/interpretability analysis correlating per-block output-embedding L2 norms with benchmark degradation, identifying blocks 28-37 (peak at block 29, r = -0.9707) as a 'second-stage amplification' corridor; (5) extend the HiSPA study to a production-scale hybrid model (Jamba-1.7-Mini, 52B) and to Mamba-2/Nemotron-3-Nano hybrids (moved to appendix H); and (6) connect HiSPA to classical Prompt Injection Attacks (PIA) by showing HiSPA-style prefixes amplify Jamba's attack success rate on the OPEN-PROMPT-INJECTIONS benchmark.
threat_model: Two explicit attacker profiles are defined in Section 3.3. (1) Z-HiSPA: zero-shot, black-box attacker with no access to model weights/gradients, crafting natural-language triggers (using 'escape', 'ignore', and 'fake completion' strategies borrowed from Liu et al. 2024's prompt-injection taxonomy) -- representing an average chatbot user. (2) M-HiSPA: multi-shot, white-box attacker with full access to model internals, optimizing triggers via a genetic algorithm against an internal loss (first-layer cosine-similarity loss, or later block-29 L2-norm maximization) -- representing an open-source community member or internal red team. The paper frames the practical risk around long-context retrieval pipelines (e.g., AI-assisted scientific literature review tools such as Scispace/Elicit) where malicious prompt-injected text could be embedded in documents, and broadens implications to agentic deployments (guardrail degradation, authorization errors, confidentiality leaks, integrity failures from attacker-skewed state).
limitations: The authors themselves state: (1) baseline clean CHSS for Mamba/Pythia is modest because non-instruction-tuned base models were deliberately chosen to isolate architectural effects, which they argue does not undermine the differential comparison but is acknowledged as a weaker demonstration than higher clean baselines would give; (2) the norm-based HiSPA detection strategy proposed in Section 7 (monitoring blocks 28-37) is NOT implemented or evaluated -- only motivated by correlation and a raw norm-range comparison (9.80-12.06 triggered vs 9.49 clean); (3) an 'optimization-performance mismatch' where GA triggers optimized on first-block loss underperform simpler Z-HiSPA triggers on the actual benchmark, requiring a second, separate GA objective (block-29 norm) to produce a stronger attack; (4) the single M-HiSPA trigger that collapses Jamba-1.7-Mini contains the <|endoftext|> special token and ALSO collapses Pythia-2.8b (a pure Transformer), so it cannot be cleanly attributed to SSM-specific dynamics -- the authors explicitly flag this as a confound and rely instead on the separate PIA experiment for clean SSM-specific evidence on Jamba; (5) the PIA re-evaluation is restricted to 3 of the original 7 OPEN-PROMPT-INJECTIONS datasets for computational reasons; (6) results for Mamba-2 and Nemotron-3-Nano were moved to an appendix (App. H) not fully covered in the main text extraction reviewed here.

datasets_used: ROBENCH-25 (introduced in this paper): 120 NeurIPS 2025 paper titles+abstracts, avg. 260.8 tokens, 2 True/False questions per abstract
The Pile (pretraining data for Mamba-2.8b and Pythia-2.8b, cited from Gao et al. 2020, not generated by this paper)
OPEN-PROMPT-INJECTIONS benchmark (Liu et al., USENIX Security 2024), used/re-evaluated with 3 of its 7 original datasets: sentiment analysis, spam detection, duplicate sentence detection
quantitative_results: Z-HiSPA: 71% average CHSS drop for Mamba-2.8b (clean vs. triggered, recovery-instruction configuration)
Z-HiSPA: 58% average CHSS increase for Pythia-2.8b under identical triggers
Table 1 CHSS values (mamba, A-R+ config): No Trigger 0.275 down to Trigger G 0.000
Table 1 CHSS values (jamba, A-R- config): stays ~0.992-1.000 across all Z-HiSPA triggers (near-immune)
Best greedy beam-search trigger (Section 3.2): first-block cosine loss of -0.997 after 6 tokens
Best GA M-HiSPA trigger (block-29 norm objective): CHSS 0.117 on ROBENCH-25, outperforming 6/7 Z-HiSPA triggers
GA trigger with <|endoftext|> special token: first-block loss -0.9998; collapses Mamba-2.8b, Pythia-2.8b, and Jamba-1.7-Mini to CHSS ~0 on ROBENCH-25
Blockwise correlation: blocks 28-37 all show r < -0.91 with CHSS; block 29 strongest at r = -0.9707
Table 2 ASV (OPEN-PROMPT-INJECTIONS): Jamba-1.7-Mini naive 0.490 -> combine 0.743 (delta +0.253, x1.516)
Table 2 ASV: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct naive 0.403 -> combine 0.510 (delta +0.107, x1.265)
Table 2 ASV: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct naive 0.272 -> combine 0.165 (delta -0.107, x0.607, i.e. attack got LESS effective)
Block-29 L2 norm: clean-prompt norm 9.49 vs. triggered-prompt norm range 9.80-12.06 (Trigger G highest at 12.06)
baselines_compared: Mamba-2.8b (state-spaces/mamba-2.8b-hf) vs. Pythia-2.8b (EleutherAI/pythia-2.8b) -- matched size/context/pretraining-data pure-SSM vs. pure-Transformer pair
Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct (pure Transformer instruction-tuned baselines for the PIA experiment)
SmolLM3-3B (mentioned as a Transformer baseline resisting the <|endoftext|> special-token trigger, alongside the two Llama models)
Mamba-2 and Nemotron-3-Nano hybrid (results deferred to Appendix H, not deeply covered in this extraction)
